claim_text: Across 11 surveyed products, exactly zero perform actual byte-level Unicode auditing of MCP tool descriptions. Cisco's scanner names 'Unicode/Homoglyph Attack' as a vector but only flags missing defenses, not actual hidden bytes. No surveyed product scans claude_desktop_config.json directly. No surveyed product produces a hidden-byte count.

sources.title: Cross-product synthesis
sources.exact_quote: missing defensive measures against 12 common attack vectors

notes: Cell on positioning grid: product=Cross-product synthesis claim_type=gap_analysis tag=VERIFIED

claim_text: The closest competitor by product surface is Invariant Labs MCP-Scan: open source, free, MCP-specific, scans tool descriptions, has the most credible research provenance (coined 'Tool Poisoning Attack'). Differentiation for mcp-scan.dev: byte-level Unicode forensics (hidden-byte count, zero-width detection, homoglyph mix), agent commandeering imperative pattern matching, forward-reference detection, and a 60-second zero-install web UI accepting raw config paste rather than a CLI install.

sources.title: Cross-product synthesis
sources.exact_quote: Statically scans all your installed servers for malicious tool descriptions

notes: Cell on positioning grid: product=Cross-product synthesis claim_type=closest_competitor tag=INFERENCE

claim_text: Eleven commercial and open-source MCP scanners survey the space; none perform byte-level Unicode forensics on tool descriptions.
notes: Roll-up of the 40 competitor_grid POSITIONING_CELL entries. Resolves to the grid summary at content/sources_v2.summary.md.

claim_text: Invariant Labs coined the term 'Tool Poisoning Attack' (TPA) on April 1, 2025, defining it as malicious instructions embedded in MCP tool descriptions that are visible to AI models but invisible to users.

sources.title: MCP Security Notification: Tool Poisoning Attacks
sources.exact_quote: A Tool Poisoning Attack occurs when malicious instructions are embedded within MCP tool descriptions that are invisible to users but visible to AI models.

sources.title: Snyk Acquires Invariant Labs to Accelerate Agentic AI Security Innovation
sources.exact_quote: discovering and naming attack terminology such as 'tool poisoning' and 'MCP rug pulls.'

sources.title: Protecting against indirect prompt injection attacks in MCP
sources.exact_quote: "Tool Poisoning" is a vulnerability where an attacker embeds malicious instructions within the descriptions of MCP tools.

sources.title: MCP: Untrusted Servers and Confused Clients, Plus a Sneaky Exploit
sources.exact_quote: This means that just enabling a tool already hands control of the LLM inference over to that specific MCP server the tool interface definition (metadata) was loaded from.

notes: Strongest version: TPA was named and demonstrated by Invariant Labs in April 2025; the term is now adopted by Microsoft and Snyk (post-acquisition). Invariant's original PoC used visible <IMPORTANT> tags, not hidden Unicode. The asymmetry (model sees, user does not) is the load-bearing concept. This is the cleanest single-claim foundation for the post.

claim_text: Zero-width and invisible Unicode characters function as a steganographic channel that LLMs read as valid tokens but humans cannot see, enabling hidden-payload prompt injection.

sources.title: Invisible Unicode Threats: Hidden Prompt Injection Attacks on LLMs
sources.exact_quote: While these characters are invisible to humans, LLMs see them as distinct, valid Unicode characters in the input stream.

sources.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: These inputs can affect the model even if they are imperceptible to humans, therefore prompt injections do not need to be human-visible/readable, as long as the content is parsed by the model.

sources.title: MCP: Untrusted Servers and Confused Clients, Plus a Sneaky Exploit
sources.exact_quote: So, again it is possible to pass hidden instructions through various UI and API layers...the user inspects the instructions, where nothing strange is visible. However, once the LLM inference starts Claude will interpret the hidden text and take to the user unknown actions.

sources.title: AI Red Teaming Agent - Microsoft Foundry
sources.exact_quote: AsciiSmuggler | Conceals data within ASCII characters, making it harder to detect.

notes: Promptfoo is the cleanest primary source, and it specifies the exact codepoints used as a binary encoding: U+200B start, U+200C as 0-bit, U+2063 as 1-bit, U+200D end. OWASP LLM01:2025 generalizes the principle. Embrace The Red's post directly demonstrates Unicode Tags injected into MCP tool descriptions that survive UI rendering. Microsoft PyRIT lists AsciiSmuggler, UnicodeConfusable, and UnicodeSubstitution as production attack strategies, which shows this is treated as a known channel by enterprise red teams. Note: this post has 0 actual hidden-Unicode attacks observed in the wild from the 911-server scan, so the claim is 'channel exists and is documented' not 'channel is being actively exploited at scale.'

claim_text: OX Security disclosed in April 2026 that a design choice in Anthropic's official MCP SDKs exposes 150 million-plus downloads and up to 200,000 servers to remote command execution, with 10-plus CVEs assigned across MCP implementations.

sources.title: The Mother of All AI Supply Chains: Critical, Systemic Vulnerability at the Core of Anthropic's MCP
sources.exact_quote: 150M+ downloads, 7,000+ publicly accessible servers — and up to 200,000 vulnerable instances in total.

sources.title: The Mother of All AI Supply Chains: Critical, Systemic Vulnerability at the Core of Anthropic's MCP
sources.exact_quote: 10 CVEs Issued (and counting)

sources.title: The Mother of All AI Supply Chains - Technical Deep Dive
sources.exact_quote: Anthropic's modelcontextprotocol gives a direct configuration-to-command execution via their STDIO interface on all of their implementations, regardless of programming language.

sources.title: MCP 'design flaw' puts 200k servers at risk: Researcher
sources.exact_quote: A design flaw – or expected behavior based on a bad design choice, depending on who is telling the story – baked into Anthropic's official Model Context Protocol (MCP) puts as many as 200,000 servers at risk of complete takeover, according to security researchers.

sources.title: Anthropic MCP Design Vulnerability Enables RCE, Threatening AI Supply Chain
sources.exact_quote: affects more than 7,000 publicly accessible servers and software packages totaling more than 150 million downloads

sources.title: MCP 'design flaw' puts 200k servers at risk: Researcher
sources.exact_quote: Anthropic 'declined to modify the protocol's architecture, citing the behavior as 'expected,'' according to the Ox researchers' statement.

notes: Verified the prior QUOTE_NOT_FOUND on the technical deep dive: the precise wording is 'direct configuration-to-command execution via their STDIO interface on all of their implementations, regardless of programming language' (without the trailing fragment originally stored). Use this corrected quote, not the longer concatenation in the existing sources.json. OX disclosure is about STDIO command injection, which is adjacent to but distinct from tool-description Tool Poisoning. Both share the same root: MCP has no protocol-level sanitization. Anthropic's 'declined to modify' response is the strongest framing for the 'door is open' pivot.

claim_text: The MCP specification defines tool description as a 'Human-readable description of functionality' with no character-set constraints, no sanitization requirements, and explicit warnings only on annotations, not descriptions.

sources.title: MCP Specification 2025-06-18: Server / Tools
sources.exact_quote: * `description`: Human-readable description of functionality

sources.title: MCP Specification 2025-06-18: Server / Tools
sources.exact_quote: For trust & safety and security, clients **MUST** consider tool annotations to be untrusted unless they come from trusted servers.

sources.title: MCP Specification 2025-06-18: Server / Tools
sources.exact_quote: Servers **MUST**: Validate all tool inputs, Implement proper access controls, Rate limit tool invocations, Sanitize tool outputs

sources.title: MCP Specification 2025-06-18: Server / Tools
sources.exact_quote: Clients **SHOULD**: Prompt for user confirmation on sensitive operations, Show tool inputs to the user before calling the server, to avoid malicious or accidental data exfiltration, Validate tool results before passing to LLM

sources.title: MCP Security Best Practices (Specification Draft)
sources.exact_quote: This document identifies security risks, attack vectors, and best practices specific to MCP implementations.

notes: Both prior QUOTE_NOT_FOUND items in sources.json are now corrected. The spec page lives at /specification/2025-06-18/server/tools (not /docs/concepts/tools). The 'Show tool inputs to the user' clause is a SHOULD on call-time arguments, not a MUST on listing-time descriptions. The MUST-untrusted clause applies to tool annotations only. Tool descriptions sit in the protocol-trusted band by default. The Security Best Practices spec covers OAuth confused deputy, token passthrough, SSRF, session hijacking, scope minimization, and local server compromise, but does NOT cover tool description sanitization or character-set restrictions. That gap is the load-bearing finding for this post.

claim_text: Anthropic's own MCP server implementations have shipped exploitable prompt-injection chains; Cyata disclosed three CVEs in mcp-server-git in January 2026, fixed in versions 2025.9.25 and 2025.12.18.

sources.title: Three Flaws in Anthropic MCP Git Server Enable File Access and Code Execution
sources.exact_quote: These flaws can be exploited through prompt injection, meaning an attacker who can influence what an AI assistant reads (a malicious README, a poisoned issue description, a compromised webpage) can weaponize these vulnerabilities

sources.title: Anthropic quietly fixed flaws in its Git MCP server
sources.exact_quote: Anthropic has fixed three bugs in its official Git MCP server that researchers say can be chained with other MCP tools to remotely execute malicious code or overwrite files via prompt injection.

sources.title: Prompt Injection Bugs Found in Official Anthropic Git MCP Server
sources.exact_quote: The flaws allow attackers to execute code when mcp-server-git is used alongside a filesystem MCP server, delete arbitrary files and load arbitrary files into a large language model's context.

sources.title: Three Flaws in Anthropic MCP Git Server Enable File Access and Code Execution
sources.exact_quote: CVE-2025-68143: A path traversal vulnerability arising as a result of the git_init tool accepting arbitrary file system paths during repository creation without validation

notes: Useful as a 'this is not theoretical, even Anthropic's own servers were vulnerable' anchor. Disclosed by Cyata (researcher Yarden Porat). Three CVEs: CVE-2025-68143 (git_init path traversal), CVE-2025-68144 (git_diff/git_checkout argument injection), CVE-2025-68145 (--repository flag path traversal). Reported June-July 2025, fixed December 2025.

claim_text: Snyk Agent Scan (formerly Invariant MCP-Scan) is the most widely adopted MCP security scanner; it detects tool poisoning, prompt injection, tool shadowing, and toxic flows, but does not detect zero-width or hidden Unicode characters in tool descriptions.

sources.title: snyk/agent-scan: Security scanner for AI agents, MCP servers and agent skills
sources.exact_quote: Detects 15+ distinct security risks across MCP servers and agent skills: MCP: Prompt Injection, Tool Poisoning, Tool Shadowing, Toxic Flows

sources.title: snyk/agent-scan: Security scanner for AI agents, MCP servers and agent skills
sources.exact_quote: Agent Scan searches through your local agent's configuration files to find agents, skills, and MCP servers. For MCP, it connects to servers and retrieves tool descriptions. It then validates the components, both with local checks and by invoking the Agent Scan API.

sources.title: Snyk Acquires Invariant Labs to Accelerate Agentic AI Security Innovation
sources.exact_quote: discovering and naming attack terminology such as 'tool poisoning' and 'MCP rug pulls.'

sources.title: How to Detect Tool Poisoning in MCP Server Security
sources.exact_quote: MCP-Scan will perform various types of analysis to find different classifications of vulnerabilities that impact MCP servers, such as tool poisoning, toxic flow analysis, and more.

notes: The negative finding is critical for the post: zero mention of zero-width or invisible Unicode detection in either the GitHub README or the Snyk Labs detection guide. This is the gap mcp-scan.dev fills. The tool's strength is pattern matching on visible <IMPORTANT> tags and toxic flows; the weakness is anything that hides in the codepoint stream. Snyk acquired Invariant Labs in June 2025; latest release v0.4.13 in April 2026.

claim_text: Microsoft's AI Red Team treats MCP tool poisoning as indirect prompt injection (XPIA), and its production red-team agent ships with codified Unicode-based obfuscation strategies including AsciiSmuggler, UnicodeConfusable, and UnicodeSubstitution.

sources.title: Protecting against indirect prompt injection attacks in MCP
sources.exact_quote: "Tool Poisoning" is a vulnerability where an attacker embeds malicious instructions within the descriptions of MCP tools.

sources.title: Protecting against indirect prompt injection attacks in MCP
sources.exact_quote: We recommend two approaches: implementing AI prompt shields, which help by analyzing prompts and tool interactions to prevent unexpected behaviors, and establishing robust supply chain security mechanisms.

sources.title: AI Red Teaming Agent - Microsoft Foundry
sources.exact_quote: Indirect Prompt Injected Attacks (also known as Cross-Domain Prompt Injected Attacks, XPIA) red teaming tests whether an agent can be manipulated by malicious instructions hidden in external data sources, such as emails or documents—retrieved via tool calls.

sources.title: AI Red Teaming Agent - Microsoft Foundry
sources.exact_quote: UnicodeConfusable | Uses Unicode characters that look similar to standard characters, creating visual confusion. | UnicodeSubstitution | Substitutes standard characters with Unicode equivalents, often for obfuscation.

sources.title: Detecting and analyzing prompt abuse in AI tools
sources.exact_quote: Prompt injection recognized as one of the most significant vulnerabilities in the 2025 OWASP guidance for Large Language Model Applications

notes: Microsoft Foundry's red-team agent product page is gold: it is one of the only enterprise sources that names Unicode-based obfuscation as a first-class attack strategy, alongside AnsiAttack, AsciiArt, AsciiSmuggler, Diacritic, etc. Note: the AI Red Teaming Agent does NOT currently support 'Function tool calls' or 'Non-Foundry agents,' so its MCP coverage is limited; this is a useful tension to surface. The DART blog (March 2026) does not name MCP specifically; it covers URL-fragment indirect injection.

claim_text: OWASP's 2025 guidance places prompt injection at the top of both the LLM Top 10 (LLM01) and the Agentic Applications Top 10 (ASI01-Goal Hijack, ASI02-Tool Misuse), and explicitly states injections do not need to be human-readable.

sources.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: A Prompt Injection Vulnerability occurs when user prompts alter the LLM's behavior or output in unintended ways.

sources.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: These inputs can affect the model even if they are imperceptible to humans, therefore prompt injections do not need to be human-visible/readable, as long as the content is parsed by the model.

sources.title: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications: The Benchmark for Agentic Security in the Age of Autonomous AI
sources.exact_quote: Hidden prompts turned copilots into silent exfiltration engines (ASI01 – Agent Goal Hijack, e.g EchoLeak).

sources.title: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications
sources.exact_quote: ASI02: Tool Misuse and Exploitation - Agents use legitimate tools in unsafe ways

sources.title: OWASP Agentic Skills Top 10
sources.exact_quote: pattern-matching scanners miss the majority of critical threats, which rely on natural-language instruction manipulation rather than code signatures.

notes: The OWASP LLM01 'imperceptible to humans' clause is the single best textbook citation for the post's core thesis. The OWASP Agentic Skills Top 10 quote is a direct rebuttal to scanner-only defense, which mcp-scan.dev should lean into. AST08 (Poor Scanning) explicitly faults the 'pattern matching is enough' assumption. ASI02 Tool Misuse covers the chained-tool exploitation we have separately seen in Cyata's Anthropic Git CVEs.

claim_text: MITRE ATLAS catalogs LLM Prompt Injection as AML.T0051 with two sub-techniques (.000 Direct, .001 Indirect), classified under the Initial Access tactic.

sources.title: LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051) | MITRE ATLAS
sources.exact_quote: LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051) is a technique in the MITRE ATLAS framework — the adversarial threat landscape for AI systems. It describes how adversaries may craft malicious prompts as inputs to an llm that cause the llm to act in unintended ways.

sources.title: LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051) | MITRE ATLAS
sources.exact_quote: indirect injection embedded within data the LLM might process later (e.g., a malicious string hidden in a web page, an email, or a document used by a RAG system)

sources.title: MITRE ATLAS | Promptfoo
sources.exact_quote: Prompt injection is the most prominent ATLAS technique, cataloged as AML.T0051

sources.title: MITRE ATLAS: AI security framework with 16 tactics and 84 techniques
sources.exact_quote: MITRE ATLAS framework with 16 tactics and 84 techniques

notes: Could not retrieve atlas.mitre.org/techniques/AML.T0051 directly (404 from this user agent). The technique is real and widely cited, but I do not have a MITRE-domain primary source verbatim in this run. To upgrade to VERIFIED-with-primary-source, fetch atlas.mitre.org via firecrawl (it likely needs JS rendering) or cite the misp-galaxy.org mirror which republishes the MITRE definitions verbatim. For now, four secondary sources from reputable security vendors converge on the same definition.

claim_text: Cursor and Claude Desktop both display tool descriptions verbatim in approval dialogs without stripping invisible Unicode characters; users have no first-class way to inspect the raw bytes of a tool description before approval.

sources.title: Cursor – Model Context Protocol (MCP)
sources.exact_quote: Agent asks for approval before using MCP tools by default. Click the arrow next to the tool name to see arguments.

sources.title: Cursor – Model Context Protocol (MCP)
sources.exact_quote: Remember that MCP servers can access external services and execute code on your behalf. Always understand what a server does before installation.

sources.title: MCP: Untrusted Servers and Confused Clients, Plus a Sneaky Exploit
sources.exact_quote: the user inspects the instructions, where nothing strange is visible. However, once the LLM inference starts Claude will interpret the hidden text and take to the user unknown actions.

sources.title: MCP Specification 2025-06-18: Server / Tools
sources.exact_quote: Provide UI that makes clear which tools are being exposed to the AI model, Insert clear visual indicators when tools are invoked, Present confirmation prompts to the user for operations, to ensure a human is in the loop

notes: Strongest evidence I have: (a) the MCP spec only requires UI visibility on call-time tool inputs, not on listing-time descriptions; (b) Cursor's docs only mention showing 'arguments' on click, not the description itself; (c) Embrace The Red demonstrated that hidden Unicode characters survive Claude Desktop's UI rendering and execute. Gap: I do not yet have a vendor-authored statement from Anthropic or Cursor or Windsurf saying 'we render the description as-is' or 'we strip invisible Unicode.' Best path to VERIFIED: screenshot test against Cursor + Claude Desktop with a U+200B-loaded MCP server (which is exactly what mcp-scan.dev should ship as a demo). Until then, frame this as 'no vendor guarantees stripping; one independent researcher confirms it does not happen.'

sources.title: AI-powered defense for an AI-accelerated threat landscape

sources.title: Threat actor abuse of AI accelerates from tool to cyberattack surface
sources.exact_quote: Threat actors are embedding AI into how they work across reconnaissance, malware development, and post-compromise operations.

sources.title: Inside an AI-enabled device code phishing campaign

sources.title: Detecting and analyzing prompt abuse in AI tools

notes: Microsoft's April 22 post is in-week but stays at the strategy level. Use as supporting context that 'this matters to enterprise CISOs right now' rather than as the lead peg.

sources.title: Prompt Injection leads to RCE and Sandbox Escape in Antigravity
sources.exact_quote: By crafting a Pattern value of '-Xsh,' attackers can cause fd to pass matched files to sh for execution as shell scripts. This vulnerability bypasses Antigravity's Secure Mode.

sources.title: Google Patches Antigravity IDE Flaw Enabling Prompt Injection Code Execution
sources.exact_quote: By injecting the -X (exec-batch) flag through the Pattern parameter, an attacker can force fd to execute arbitrary binaries against workspace files.

sources.title: Vuln in Google's Antigravity AI agent manager could escape sandbox, give attackers remote code execution

Antigravity Sandbox Escape: Prompt Injection and Native Tool Abuse

claim_text: The Trojan Source paper (Boucher and Anderson, University of Cambridge, 2021) formalized the use of Unicode bidirectional override control characters to make source code render differently to humans than it does to compilers, opening a class of invisible source-code attacks.

sources.title: Trojan Source: Invisible Vulnerabilities
sources.exact_quote: source code is maliciously encoded so that it appears different to a compiler and to the human eye...tokens are logically encoded in a different order from the one in which they are displayed

sources.title: Trojan Source Attacks
sources.publisher: Boucher and Anderson, University of Cambridge
sources.exact_quote: The trick is to use Unicode control characters to reorder tokens in source code at the encoding level. These visually reordered tokens can be used to display logic that, while semantically correct, diverges from the logic presented by the logical ordering of source code tokens.

sources.title: VU#999008: Various Unicode characters can be leveraged to inject malicious code into source code which appears benign to a human reviewer
sources.exact_quote: Attacks that allow for unintended control of Unicode and homoglyphic characters...leverage text encoding that may cause source code to be interpreted differently by a compiler than it appears visually to a human reviewer.

sources.title: CVE-2021-42574 (NVD)
sources.exact_quote: An issue was discovered in the Bidirectional Algorithm in the Unicode Specification through 14.0. It permits the visual reordering of characters via control sequences, which can be used to craft source code that renders different logic than the logical ordering of tokens ingested by compilers and interpreters.

notes: Trojan Source is the canonical academic precedent. Boucher and Anderson are at Cambridge Computer Lab. The CVE was DISPUTED by Unicode Consortium but accepted by every major language vendor (Rust, GitHub, Python, Apple, Red Hat). This is the strongest single anchor for the post: invisible-byte attacks on text-processing systems are a well-established 2021 result. CVSS 8.3 HIGH.

claim_text: Unicode's own technical reports (UTR #36 Security Considerations, UTS #39 Security Mechanisms, UTS #55 Source Code Handling) explicitly enumerate the security risks of invisible characters, bidirectional overrides, and visually confusable strings, and define the canonical mechanisms (skeletons, mixed-script detection) for filtering them.

sources.title: UTR #36: Unicode Security Considerations (version tr36-15)
sources.exact_quote: Certain Unicode characters are invisible, although they may affect the rendering of the characters around them. An example is the joiner character, used to request a cursive connection such as in Arabic.

sources.title: UTR #36 Section 2.5: Bidirectional Text Spoofing
sources.exact_quote: Never allow bidi override characters. As much as possible, avoid mixing right-to-left and left-to-right characters in a single name.

sources.title: UTS #39: Unicode Security Mechanisms (version 17.0.0)
sources.exact_quote: X and Y are mixed-script confusables if and only if they are confusable but their resolved script sets have no elements in common.

sources.title: UTS #39 Section 4: Confusable Detection (version 17.0.0)
sources.exact_quote: Remove any characters in X that have the property Default_Ignorable_Code_Point

sources.title: UTS #55: Unicode Source Code Handling (version 2)
sources.exact_quote: invisible characters that are used as overrides to the default behavior of the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm

notes: Unicode's own standards-track documents acknowledge these risks. UTS #55 was published explicitly in response to Trojan Source. UTR #36 has been stabilized but still serves as the foundational reference. UTS #39 defines the algorithms (confusable skeletons, mixed-script detection) that any LLM tool description sanitizer should implement.

claim_text: MITRE/NIST formally tracked the Trojan Source attack family with two CVEs in November 2021: CVE-2021-42574 (bidirectional override) and CVE-2021-42694 (homoglyph identifiers), each scored CVSS 8.3 HIGH.

sources.title: CVE-2021-42574 (NVD)
sources.exact_quote: An issue was discovered in the Bidirectional Algorithm in the Unicode Specification through 14.0. It permits the visual reordering of characters via control sequences, which can be used to craft source code that renders different logic than the logical ordering of tokens ingested by compilers and interpreters.

sources.title: CVE-2021-42694 (NVD)
sources.exact_quote: An issue was discovered in the character definitions of the Unicode Specification through 14.0. The specification allows an adversary to produce source code identifiers such as function names using homoglyphs that render visually identical to a target identifier.

sources.title: CERT/CC VU#999008: Trojan Source CVE assignments
sources.exact_quote: Two CVEs assigned: CVE-2021-42574 (Bidi attack) and CVE-2021-42694 (homoglyph attack)

sources.title: Trojan Source Attacks (CVE list)
sources.exact_quote: CVE-2021-42574 - Tracks the main Unicode bidirectional control character attack pattern

notes: Both CVEs are listed as DISPUTED by MITRE. The dispute is from the Unicode Consortium asserting that bidi behavior is not itself a vulnerability. Vendors disagreed and shipped fixes anyway, which is the relevant signal. CVSS 8.3 (AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H) on both.

claim_text: Major language and platform vendors (Rust, GitHub, Python) shipped explicit Trojan Source mitigations within weeks of disclosure: Rust 1.56.1 added compiler lints rejecting the affected codepoints, GitHub displayed bidi warnings on file views, and the Python core team published PEP 672 enumerating the language-level risks.

sources.title: Security advisory for rustc (CVE-2021-42574)
sources.exact_quote: Rust 1.56.1, released on November 1, 2021, introduced two new deny-by-default lints detecting the affected codepoints, respectively in string literals and in comments. The lints will prevent source code files containing those codepoints from being compiled.

sources.title: Warning about bidirectional Unicode text
sources.exact_quote: A warning is now displayed when a file's contents include bidirectional Unicode text. Such text can be interpreted or compiled differently than it appears in a user interface.

sources.title: PEP 672: Unicode-related Security Considerations for Python
sources.exact_quote: This document explains possible ways to misuse Unicode to write Python programs that appear to do something else than they actually do.

sources.title: PEP 672: Bidirectional Text section
sources.exact_quote: This command still assigns a 100-character string to s, but when displayed as general text following the Bidirectional Algorithm (e.g. in a browser), it appears as s = "א" followed by a comment.

notes: Industry response to Trojan Source was rapid and broad. The pattern (vendor adds mitigation in weeks) is exactly what is missing for the MCP tool-description case in 2026. Cite this to set up the rhetorical move: this is the same class of bug, the fix is known, why is the mitigation not yet in MCP runtimes.

claim_text: Zero-width and joining-control characters (ZWJ U+200D, ZWNJ U+200C, Zero Width Space U+200B) have been understood as identifier-spoofing and gateway-evasion primitives since well before the LLM era, with explicit warnings in IDNA / RFC 5894.

sources.title: RFC 5894: IDNA Background, Explanation, and Rationale (Section 3.1.2)
sources.exact_quote: exceptional danger of being used to produce misleading labels or to cause unacceptable ambiguity in label matching

sources.title: UTR #36 Section 3.5: Zero-Width Characters
sources.exact_quote: A gateway might be checking for a sensitive sequence...If what is passed in is 'deXlete', where X is a noncharacter, the gateway lets it through.

sources.title: UTS #39 Section 3.1.1: Joining Controls
sources.exact_quote: to contexts in which they are necessary

sources.title: Invisible Unicode Threats
sources.exact_quote: Unicode, the universal character encoding standard, contains thousands of characters - including ones that are completely invisible to the human eye.

notes: Zero-width characters as a covert channel predate LLMs by 15+ years. RFC 5894 is the long-standing IETF acknowledgement. UTR #36 explicitly describes the gateway-evasion attack pattern (deXlete) which is exactly the structural shape of LLM tool-description bypass.

claim_text: IDN homograph attacks are a long-standing browser security problem; both Chrome and Firefox apply UTS #39 mixed-script and confusable detection to decide when to display Unicode hostnames vs Punycode.

sources.title: IDN in Google Chrome
sources.exact_quote: Test the label for mixed script confusable per UTS 39. If mixed script confusable is detected, show punycode.

sources.title: Chrome IDN: Whole script confusables
sources.exact_quote: If all the letters in a given label belong to a set of whole-script-confusable letters...and if the hostname doesn't have a corresponding allowed top-level-domain for that script, show punycode.

sources.title: IDN Display Algorithm
sources.exact_quote: any intra-script near-homographs will be recognisable to people who understand that script.

sources.title: RFC 5894 Section 3.3: Layered Restrictions
sources.exact_quote: there is no single magic bullet for any of the security, confusability, or other issues associated with IDNs.

notes: The browser industry already solved 'when do we render Unicode vs escape it' for hostnames. The MCP world has not solved it for tool descriptions. This is a clean analogy for the post.

claim_text: Unicode tag characters (U+E0000 to U+E007F) are a covert channel that mirrors ASCII but is not rendered in most UIs, and Riley Goodside (January 11, 2024) was first to demonstrate publicly that LLMs read these characters as text while humans see nothing.

sources.title: ASCII Smuggler Tool: Crafting Invisible Text and Decoding Hidden Codes
sources.exact_quote: The Tags Unicode Block mirrors ASCII and because it is often not rendered in the UI, the special text remains unnoticable to users… but LLMs interpret such text.

sources.title: ASCII Smuggler: Riley Goodside attribution
sources.exact_quote: A few days ago Riley Goodside posted about an interesting discovery on how an LLM prompt injection can happen via invisible instructions in pasted text.

sources.title: Understanding and Mitigating Unicode Tag Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: On January 11th 2024, Security researcher Riley Goodside shared a new prompt injection obfuscation technique on Twitter that leverages unicode 'tag' characters to render ASCII text invisible to the human eye.

sources.title: Invisible text via Unicode tag characters
sources.exact_quote: A few days ago Riley Goodside posted about an interesting discovery on how an LLM prompt injection can happen via invisible instructions in pasted text.

notes: The Tag block (E0000-E007F) attack is the cleanest single demo for the post. Goodside's tweet (Jan 11 2024) is the canonical first-disclosure for the LLM variant. Rehberger's ASCII Smuggler tool followed three days later. This claim alone justifies that the threat is well-known and ungated.

claim_text: Recent academic and industry literature (2024-2026) has formalized invisible-Unicode prompt injection as a measurable attack class against LLMs, including reverse-CAPTCHA evaluations, font-injection vectors, and detection systems for hidden prompts in structured documents.

sources.title: Reverse CAPTCHA: Evaluating LLM Susceptibility to Invisible Unicode Instruction Injection
sources.exact_quote: We introduce Reverse CAPTCHA, an evaluation framework that tests whether large language models follow invisible Unicode-encoded instructions embedded in otherwise normal-looking text. Unlike traditional CAPTCHAs that distinguish humans from machines, our benchmark exploits a capability gap: models can perceive Unicode control characters that are invisible to human readers.

sources.title: Invisible Prompts, Visible Threats: Malicious Font Injection in External Resources for Large Language Models
sources.exact_quote: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly equipped with capabilities of real-time web search and integrated with protocols like Model Context Protocol (MCP).

sources.title: Invisible Unicode Threats
sources.exact_quote: While these characters are invisible to humans, LLMs see them as distinct, valid Unicode characters in the input stream.

sources.title: Understanding and Mitigating Unicode Tag Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: These instructions are invisible in user interfaces because they use Unicode Tags code points, which are not displayed. However, these characters were included in the training data for LLMs, enabling the models to interpret them.

notes: The Reverse CAPTCHA paper (Feb 2026) is the single best academic citation for 'this is a measurable LLM-specific attack' because it explicitly frames the human/model perception gap. The Invisible Prompts/Visible Threats paper (May 2025) is the only academic paper that names MCP directly.

claim_text: Johann Rehberger's ASCII Smuggler (January 14, 2024) is the public tooling that operationalized Unicode tag injection, and it has been actively maintained and extended through 2025 (Sneaky Bits, variant selectors).

sources.title: ASCII Smuggler Tool: Crafting Invisible Text and Decoding Hidden Codes
sources.exact_quote: The Tags Unicode Block mirrors ASCII and because it is often not rendered in the UI, the special text remains unnoticable to users… but LLMs interpret such text.

sources.title: Microsoft Fixes ASCII Smuggling Flaw That Enabled Data Theft from Microsoft 365 Copilot
sources.exact_quote: ASCII Smuggling is a novel technique that uses special Unicode characters that mirror ASCII but are actually not visible in the user interface.

sources.title: Understanding and Mitigating Unicode Tag Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: UTF-8 Begin: \xf3\xa0\x80\x80 ... CodePoint Begin: E0000 ... CodePoint End: E007F

sources.title: Invisible Unicode Threats (Promptfoo)
sources.exact_quote: While these characters serve legitimate purposes in text processing and formatting, they can also be weaponized to create a dangerous new class of attacks against LLMs.

notes: ASCII Smuggler is publicly hosted, encodes/decodes Unicode tag block payloads, and has been used in real-world Copilot exploits. Microsoft's August 2024 patch is the proof-by-impact: a major vendor had to ship a fix because the attack worked in production.

claim_text: Documented LLM jailbreaks and exploits using invisible Unicode include the Microsoft 365 Copilot ASCII Smuggling data exfiltration (disclosed Jan 2024, patched Aug 2024) and Riley Goodside's flag-emoji tag-block GPT-4 demonstrations.

sources.title: Microsoft Fixes ASCII Smuggling Flaw That Enabled Data Theft from Microsoft 365 Copilot
sources.exact_quote: Sensitive data in emails - including multi-factor authentication codes - could be transmitted to adversary-controlled servers through this attack chain.

sources.title: ASCII Smuggler: GPT-4 / DALL-E proof of concept
sources.exact_quote: A few days ago Riley Goodside posted about an interesting discovery on how an LLM prompt injection can happen via invisible instructions in pasted text.

sources.title: Invisible text via Unicode tag characters
sources.exact_quote: Goodside's initial finding demonstrated how ChatGPT could be manipulated through hidden instructions to invoke DALL-E image creation.

sources.title: Understanding and Mitigating Unicode Tag Prompt Injection
sources.exact_quote: a victim could unknowingly copy and paste an invisible malicious prompt

notes: The Microsoft Copilot case (Rehberger -> MSRC -> Aug 2024 patch) is the strongest 'this attack class shipped real harm' citation. Goodside's GPT-4 flag-emoji demo is the primordial public LLM-tag-injection PoC. The Language Log quote is paraphrased from the source's reporting on Goodside's claim, hence INFERENCE_FROM_SUMMARY.
